narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much effort and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, which is below the typical student's 5.71; a notably lower Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the average 5.53; lower-middle family income versus the dataset's average bracket of 3.47; an LSAT score of 30 against the usual 36.7; an undergraduate GPA of 2.9 below the average 3.21; and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for 92.7% of students. The model assessed your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try at 56%, tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection—meaning it predicted a higher risk of not passing compared to those approved. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile, as it stood out as much weaker than what the model sees in students who typically pass. Other elements, like your Year 1 decile, family income, undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and full-time status, all worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing the bar, but they weren't strong enough to outweigh the Year 3 concern. In comparison to typical students in the dataset—who often have stronger deciles, higher test scores, and GPAs—your Year 3 performance was the key area where you differed most significantly from successful profiles. This doesn't reflect your full potential or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers, but rather how these metrics aligned with past bar exam outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or strengthening your recent academics through additional coursework or a post-bac program to boost your decile rankings. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique background more heavily. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant experience could help reframe your story in future applications. We're rooting for you—many have turned similar predictions around with targeted improvements. Keep pushing; your journey is far from over.
